title: Exercises: Tokens & Tokenization
description: Hands-on practice problems to reinforce learning
instructions: These exercises are optional and self-checked. Try them after completing the module to practice your skills.

exercises.title: Estimate Token Count for Different Texts
exercises.description: Use the 0.75 heuristic to estimate token count for various texts
exercises.prompt: For each text below, estimate the number of tokens using the heuristic that 1 token ≈ 0.75 words.

texts.content: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.

Natural language processing is a field of artificial intelligence that focuses on the interaction between computers and human language. It's used in applications like translation, sentiment analysis, and chatbots.
Hello, how are you? I'm doing well, thank you for asking! What brings you here today?

text1.explanation: A short sentence with 9 words becomes approximately 12 tokens

text2.explanation: A longer paragraph with 36 words becomes approximately 48 tokens

text3.explanation: Multiple sentences with contractions and punctuation (~25 tokens)

exercises.title: Will This Fit in the Context Window?
exercises.description: Practical exercises on planning input size for different models
exercises.prompt: For each scenario, determine if the text will fit in the model's context window.

scenarios.question: Will the entire paper fit in one request?

solution.explanation: 8,000 tokens > 4,096 token limit. The paper will be truncated and GPT-3.5 will only see the first ~3,000 words.
66,667 tokens < 200,000 token limit. The entire contract will fit with room to spare.

scenarios.question: How much of the article can the model see?

solution.explanation: 3,333 tokens < 4,096 token limit. The entire article fits, with about 750 tokens to spare for the model's response.

exercises.title: Tokenizer Exploration
exercises.description: Compare how different tokenizers handle the same text (code-based, optional)
exercises.prompt: If you have access to Python and the transformers library, try tokenizing text with different tokenizers and see how they differ.
exercises.code_template: Don't worry, I can help you with natural language processing!
exercises.expected_observation: Different tokenizers will produce different token counts for the same text. GPT-2 might produce 13-15 tokens, BERT might produce 16-18, and LLaMA might produce 14-16 tokens.

solution.key_insight: Each model comes with its own tokenizer, and they tokenize differently
solution.explanation: This demonstrates that token count is tokenizer-specific. You must use the correct tokenizer for your model.
solution.practical_implication: If you're using multiple models, be aware that the same input will have different token counts for each one

exercises.title: Plan a Real Project
exercises.description: Plan token requirements for a real-world application
exercises.prompt: Pick a task and estimate token requirements:

task_ideas.name: Document summarization system
questions: What's the typical document length (in words)?
How many tokens will that be?
Which models have sufficient context? (check their max context)

task_ideas.name: Multi-turn conversation chatbot
questions: Each turn might be ~100 words (user + assistant)
Is 4,096 tokens enough for a 10-turn conversation?

task_ideas.name: Code analysis tool
questions: A typical file is 500 lines of code
Estimate tokens (code might be token-heavier than prose)
How much context is needed for good analysis?

solution.approach: Estimate total input + buffer for response
solution.formula: Input tokens + response tokens < context window
solution.example: If you need 3,000 tokens for input and want 1,000 tokens for response, you need a 4,000+ context window

hints_and_tips: The 0.75 heuristic is approximate—actual count depends on language and tokenizer
Always leave buffer space in context window for the model's response
When in doubt, use the actual tokenizer to count (more accurate than heuristic)
Different languages have different token-to-word ratios
Code and numbers may tokenize differently than natural language
